statement: Sjálfstæðisflokkurinn (Independence Party) introduced a bill in Althingi proposing an 18-month cooling-off period that would prevent senior officials and ministerial advisors involved in negotiations with international organisations from accepting positions with those organisations after leaving office. The bill was introduced in the context of potential EU accession negotiations, framed as a safeguard against conflicts of interest. Similar 'revolving door' regulations exist at the EU institutional level: EU Commissioners face an 18-month cooling-off period (extended to 24 months for the Commission President), and the European Ombudsman has called for stricter enforcement. Several EU member states, including France and Germany, have analogous rules for senior civil servants.
source_name: Alþingi bill database; EU Staff Regulations Art. 16; European Ombudsman recommendations
caveats: The bill's exact status in Althingi (whether it has been debated, referred to committee, or voted on) requires verification from the Althingi bill database. The comparison with EU-level cooling-off periods is contextual — the Icelandic bill's scope and enforcement mechanisms may differ substantially.

related_entries.statement: The EU's Common Security and Defence Policy (CSDP) operates alongside but does not replace NATO. Article 42(2) TEU states that EU defence policy 'shall not prejudice the specific character of the security and defence policy' of NATO members. Iceland is a founding NATO member (1949) but has no standing military, relying instead on the Icelandic Coast Guard and bilateral defence agreements. EU membership would involve participation in CSDP missions and the European Defence Agency, but would not require establishing armed forces. Six EU members (Austria, Cyprus, Finland, Ireland, Malta, Sweden — though Finland and Sweden joined NATO in 2023–2024) have historically maintained varying forms of military non-alignment.

Denmark negotiated opt-outs from the euro, defence, justice/home affairs, and EU citizenship provisions in the 1992 Edinburgh Agreement. Ireland has opt-outs from Schengen and justice/home affairs. Poland and Czech Republic have protocols on the Charter of Fundamental Rights. Whether Iceland could negotiate similar opt-outs during accession is a political question with no guaranteed outcome.

citations.report_title: Flott embætti í boði fyrir ESB ríki

claims.claim_text: Aðildarríki ESB eigi tilkall til ýmissa embætta innan stofnana sambandsins og sagan sýni að æðstu embættin séu gjarnan skipuð fyrrum ráðherrum og háttsettum embættismönnum.
